ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a practical, engineering-focused method (L1-norm ranking to prune entire convolutional filters, one-shot network-wide pruning and retraining, and ResNet-specific handling/sensitivity analysis—Sections 3.1, 3.3–3.4, 4.2–4.3). While the paper clearly differentiates itself from prior weight-level magnitude pruning and group-sparse regularization and compares to activation-based channel pruning (Related Work and Sections 2, 4.5), the core idea—structured pruning of filters based on magnitude—is an incremental and unsurprising extension of existing pruning and group-sparsity ideas (Han et al., Lebedev et al., Zhou et al.), rather than a fundamentally new formulation or theoretical advance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is overall well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly presents its contributions early (Intro claims filter pruning, one-shot pruning, and sensitivity analysis). Notation in Section 3 is defined and used consistently (e.g., Fi,j, xi, hi/wi and the FLOP formula), and the method steps are described concretely. Figures/tables are repeatedly referenced and appear informative for the sensitivity and pruning results, though the actual figures/tables are not present here; presentation suffers from formatting issues (duplicated reference blocks at the end), a few typographical errors (e.g., “hayperparameters”) and some cramped inline equations that slightly reduce readability. Overall the writing is clear with only minor presentation problems that merit cleanup.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (L1-based filter pruning yields structured compression with ~30%+ FLOP reduction and recoverable accuracy via retraining) are clearly stated and supported by experiments and sensible analysis (see operation count in Sec.3, L1 selection in Sec.3.1, layer sensitivity and empirical results in Sec.4 for VGG-16 and ResNets). Mathematical arguments are minimal but correct (cost reduction when removing filters is computed correctly). The experimental methodology is generally sound (baselines include random/largest and activation-based criteria, full training-set statistics for comparisons, FLOP and wall-clock measures reported), but lacks some rigor: retraining schedules are short, comparisons to other structured-pruning or regularization baselines are limited, and no statistical variability or significance testing is reported. Overall the technical approach and evidence are convincing though experimental thoroughness and some controls could be improved.
